description: Balanites aegyptiaca is a species of tree, classified as a member of either the Zygophyllaceae or the Balanitaceae. This tree is native to much of Africa and parts of the Middle East. It can be found in many kinds of habitat, tolerating a wide variety of soil types, from sand to heavy clay, and climatic moisture levels, from arid to subhumid. It is relatively tolerant of flooding, livestock activity, and wildfire.
origin: CVI, GAM OO, YEM, BEN OO, MOZ

care.tips: Native to: MLW OO, OMA OO, ARABIAN PENINSULA, GHA OO, ALG

description: Guaiacum coulteri is a species of flowering plant in the family Zygophyllaceae, that is native to western Mexico and Guatemala.

Guaiacum officinale, commonly known as roughbark lignum-vitae, guaiacwood or gaïacwood, is a species of tree in the caltrop family, Zygophyllaceae, that is native to the Caribbean and the northern coast of South America.
origin: ARU OO, NLA, DOM OO, WIN DO, CUB

description: Guaiacum sanctum, commonly known as holywood, lignum vitae or holywood lignum-vitae, is a species of flowering plant in the creosote bush family, Zygophyllaceae. It is native to the Neotropical realm, from Mexico through Central America, Florida in the United States, the Caribbean, and northern South America. It has been introduced to other tropical areas of the world.
origin: BAH, BLZ, PUE, TRT, MXS GR

description: Kallstroemia grandiflora, the Arizona poppy, is a species of summer annual herb inhabiting the deserts of the Southwestern United States, California, and northern Mexico. It is not related to true poppies.
origin: MXN, MXN SO, MXG, MXE AG, MXS GR
synonyms: ["Tribulus grandiflorus", "Kallstroemia grandiflora var. detonsa", "Tribulus fisheri", "Kallstroemia grandiflora var. arizonica"]

origin: NIC, MXC TL, TRT OO, CUB, LEE AV
synonyms: ["Kallstroemia tribulus", "Tribulus maximus", "Kallstroemia canescens", "Tribulus dimidiatus", "Tribulus decolor", "Tribulus tuberculatus", "Tribulus terrestris", "Tribulus trijugatus"]

description: Kallstroemia parviflora, also called small-flowered carpetweed or warty caltrop, is a species of flowering plant. It is found in the United States, and extends as far south as Central America. From east to west, it can be found from Illinois to Arizona. It is an orange or yellow small-flowered annual, with zygomorphic flowers consistent with the Zygophyllaceae, or creosote-bush family, to which it belongs. Leaves are compound and are 3–6 cm, with 4 pairs leaflets.
origin: MRY OO, MXE AG, UTA, MXS GR, ARI OO

description: Larrea divaricata, commonly known as chaparral, is a small evergreen bush in the family Zygophyllaceae. It is native to arid regions of South America, where it is known as jarilla or jarillo. It was first described in 1800 by the Spanish botanist Antonio José Cavanilles.
origin: AGE FO, AGW SL, AGS RN, CLC LA, AGS
synonyms: ["Larrea divaricata f. viscosa", "Neoschroetera divaricata", "Larrea divaricata f. subsericea", "Schroeterella divaricata", "Larrea monticellii", "Covillea divaricata"]

description: Larrea tridentata, also called creosote bush, greasewood, and chaparral is a medicinal herb. In Sonora, it is more commonly called hediondilla; Spanish hediondo = "smelly". It is a flowering plant in the family Zygophyllaceae. The specific name tridentata refers to its three-toothed leaves.
origin: Southwest to South Central United States to Mexico
synonyms: ["Larrea divaricata var. arenaria", "Larrea mexicana", "Neoschroetera tridentata", "Schroeterella glutinosa", "Larrea glutinosa", "Schroeterella tridentata", "Guaiacum mexicanum", "Zygophyllum tridentatum", "Covillea glutinosa", "Covillea tridentata"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: desert or dry shrubland
care.toxicity_note: Non-toxic to Dogs, Non-Toxic to Cats, Non-Toxic to Horses.
care.used_for: ["An important medicine in many indigenous cultures of the desert southwest."]
care.used_for_details: An important medicine in many indigenous cultures of the desert southwest.

ja: クレオソートブッシュ

description: Guaiacum angustifolium is a species of flowering plant in the caltrop family, Zygophyllaceae. Common names include Texas guaiacum, Texas lignum-vitae, soapbush and huayacán. It is native to southern and western Texas in the United States and northern Mexico. The specific name is derived from the Latin angustus, meaning "narrow," and -folius, meaning "-leaved".
Roepera similis is a succulent annual herb native to Australia.

Tribulus cistoides, also called wanglo, the Jamaican feverplant or puncture vine, is a species of flowering plant in the family Zygophyllaceae, which is widely distributed in tropical and subtropical regions.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) May have been used in herbal medicine. Currently used a dietary supplement
care.edible_parts: The leaves and shoots are eaten in Asia

description: Tribulus terrestris is an annual plant in the caltrop family (Zygophyllaceae) widely distributed around the world. It is adapted to thrive in dry climate locations in which few other plants can survive.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) May have been used in herbal medicine. Currently used a dietary supplement
care.edible_parts: The leaves and shoots are eaten in Asia
